  • Source Han Serif + Playfair Display (for an elegant feel)
Clean typography — unified fonts, clear hierarchy

docs.title: Size Hierarchy
docs.text:
A typical cover carries 2-3 layers of text. The sizes need to be different enough that the viewer instantly knows what to read first.

Level Purpose Size guideline Weight Headline The core message — seen first Roughly 1/5 to 1/4 of the image width Bold / Heavy Subheading Supporting info, adds context 50%-60% of the headline size Regular / Medium Details Tags, dates, minor info 30%-40% of the headline size Light / Regular 5 Tips to Make Your Cover 10x Better From composition to color — all in one guide Master these fundamentals and your posts will look instantly more polished...

The 1.5x rule

Keep at least a 1.5x size ratio between your headline and subheading. Anything less and they look like they're competing at the same level.

✗ Avoid Three fonts on one cover!!! And yet another for the subtitle ✓ Recommended Clean, bold headline Subtitle in the same font family

Avoid size chaos

Some covers end up with four or five different text sizes scattered around with no clear logic. Rule of thumb: three size levels max. Less is more.

docs.title: Text Layout
docs.text: With fonts and sizes locked in, the next question is where and how to place the text.

docs.title: Alignment
docs.text:
• Center-aligned — Suits symmetrical compositions, poster-style covers, and formal content
  • Left-aligned — The most natural reading direction; works well when there's a lot of text
  • Right-aligned — Can feel distinctive in small doses, but avoid it as your primary alignment

The golden rule of alignment

Whatever alignment you choose, stick with one per cover. Mixing center and left alignment is one of the most common beginner mistakes — it makes the entire layout feel disjointed.

docs.title: Line Spacing and Letter Spacing
docs.text:
• Line spacing — Set it to 1.2-1.5x the font size. Too tight feels cramped; too loose feels disconnected.
  • Letter spacing — Default spacing is usually fine for body text. For headlines, slightly increased letter spacing adds breathing room and a premium feel.

docs.title: Text and Image Relationship
docs.text: Cover text isn't a sticker. It needs to live with the image, not just sit on top of it.

docs.title: Place Text in Natural Whitespace
docs.text: The ideal approach: plan for text placement when you shoot or select your image. Sky, walls, tabletops — these natural blank areas are your text's best friends.

docs.title: Semi-Transparent Backdrops
docs.text:
If the image doesn't offer clean whitespace, add a semi-transparent overlay behind the text:

  • White overlay — Fits light, fresh aesthetics
  • Black overlay — Suits dark, moody, or premium-feeling covers
  • Tinted overlay — Matches the image's dominant color for a cohesive look

Opacity sweet spot

Aim for 50%-70% opacity. Too transparent and the text is hard to read; too opaque and it hides the image behind it.

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
Steer clear of these

  • More than 2 fonts — One Chinese + one English is your maximum. More than that reads as chaotic, not creative.
  • Unclear size hierarchy — When every line of text is roughly the same size, nothing stands out.
  • Over-decoration — Outlines, drop shadows, gradients, 3D effects… skip them. Clean text ages best.
  • Text covering the subject — Words plastered over a face or product defeat the purpose of the image.
  • Too many text colors — Stick to two colors at most, both in harmony with the overall palette.

docs.title: Takeaways
docs.text:
Great typography on a cover boils down to three ideas:

  1. Match the font to the mood — let the typeface reinforce the content's personality
  2. Create clear hierarchy — size and weight should leave no doubt about what to read first
  3. Exercise restraint — fewer fonts, fewer colors, fewer effects, always

Next up, we'll cover information hierarchy and layout — connecting composition and typography into a complete cover design.

docs.title: Part 2: Color
docs.text: Color is the heart of visual appeal on Xiaohongshu. A well-chosen palette instantly communicates your style and mood, making users pause mid-scroll to look at your cover. This part takes you from color theory fundamentals to trending palettes, matching rules, and hands-on tools for building your own color schemes.

docs.title: 2.1 Color Theory
docs.text:
The color wheel, HSB model, color temperature, and color psychology — understanding these fundamentals is the first step toward great palettes. You don't need to memorize hex codes, but you do need to understand why certain colors work.

Key concepts: Color wheel, primary & complementary colors, HSB model, color temperature, color psychology

docs.title: 2.2 Style Palettes
docs.text:
From Morandi tones to creamy pastels, from high-saturation contrast to dark & gold — curated palettes organized by style, ready to use. Each palette comes with hex codes and recommended use cases.

Key concepts: Morandi palette, high-saturation contrast, cream tones, dark & gold

docs.title: 2.3 Matching Rules
docs.text:
The 60-30-10 rule, monochromatic gradients, complementary contrast — master these principles to create palettes that are both harmonious and layered. We also cover common pitfalls to help you avoid rookie mistakes.

Key concepts: 60-30-10 rule, monochromatic matching, complementary contrast, common pitfalls

docs.title: 2.4 Tools & Practice
docs.text:
A roundup of great color tools, from mobile apps to online platforms. Plus a step-by-step workflow for applying everything you've learned to every post you create.

Key concepts: Xingtu, Canva, Coolors, Adobe Color, Butter Camera

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text:
We recommend reading the chapters in order, building from theory to practice:

2.1 Color Theory (foundations)
    ↓
2.2 Style Palettes (inspiration library)
    ↓
2.3 Matching Rules (methodology)
    ↓
2.4 Tools & Practice (execution)

Tip

Short on time? Jump straight to 2.2 Style Palettes to pick a palette that fits your style, then circle back to the theory later.

docs.title: 2.3 Matching Rules
docs.text:
You've picked your palette — now it's time to distribute those colors across your canvas. Good color matching isn't about piling attractive colors together randomly; it's about proportion, hierarchy, and intention. This chapter covers the most practical matching rules and the pitfalls you must avoid.

✗ Color disaster Today's Share Super cute outfit! Wore something really cute today New User ♡ 3 ✓ 60-30-10 rule Fall/Winter Workwear Soft yet elevated color palettes This fall palette is everything | Work & date Style Guide ♡ 8.3k

docs.title: The 60-30-10 Rule
docs.text:
The most classic color proportion rule in design — simple yet remarkably effective.

The 60-30-10 Rule

  • 60% Dominant color: The large-area base, usually the background. Sets the overall tone.
  • 30% Secondary color: Used for supporting elements like color blocks, decorations, and subheadings. Adds depth.
  • 10% Accent color: The most eye-catching color, reserved for titles, buttons, and key information. The finishing touch.

Applied examples:

Element Ratio Morandi Scheme Dark & Gold Scheme Background 60% Milk Tea #C8B49A Rich Black #1A1A1A Blocks/Decor 30% Dusty Blue #8EA4B5 Charcoal #333333 Titles/Focus 10% Rose Beige #C4A882 Gold #C9A96E 60% Dominant 30% Secondary 10% Organization Guide

60-30-10 Rule in Action

60% Cream 30% Pink 10%

A Universal Trick

Not sure if your proportions are right? Squint at your finished cover. If you can clearly sense "one large area of base color + some supporting color + a pop of brightness," your ratios are on track.

docs.title: Monochromatic Matching
docs.text:
Monochromatic matching is the safest, most foolproof approach.

What is monochromatic? A set of colors derived from a single hue by varying its saturation and brightness. Light blue, medium blue, and dark blue form a monochromatic set.

Advantages:

  • Naturally harmonious — nearly impossible to look bad
  • Creates a unified, clean, polished appearance
  • Perfect for maintaining a consistent grid aesthetic

How to do it:

  1. Pick a base hue (e.g., blue)
  2. Use the lightest shade as your background
  3. Use the mid-tone for supporting elements
  4. Use the darkest shade for text or titles

Monochromatic Example

Blue family: pale grey-blue (background) → dusty blue (color blocks) → deep navy (titles). Three shades from the same hue, automatically harmonious.

Watch out for:

  • Ensure at least 3 levels of light-dark variation; otherwise the design will look flat
  • Add small amounts of neutral colors (white, grey, beige) as transitions

docs.title: Complementary Matching
docs.text:
Complementary colors sit far apart on the color wheel — typically directly opposite. Pairing them creates bold visual tension.

Classic complementary combos:

Combo Visual Effect Use Case Red + Green Festive, energetic Needs a buffer color Blue + Orange Modern, sporty Tech, fitness Purple + Yellow Luxurious, edgy Streetwear, art

The Key to Complementary Pairing

Done well, complementary colors look like intentional, high-end contrast. Done poorly, they're an eyesore. The secrets:

  1. Unequal area: Let one color dominate; use the other as an accent
  2. Add buffers: Separate the two with white, grey, or beige
  3. Desaturate one: If both colors are vivid, dial back the saturation on one of them

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text: These are the most frequent color disasters on Xiaohongshu. Steer clear.

docs.title: Pitfall 1: Large Areas of Neon
docs.text:
Neon green, neon pink, neon yellow — covering large areas with neon colors makes designs look cheap and straining on the eyes.

Neon Isn't Banned

Neon can work beautifully as a small-area element in titles or icons. Just never use it as a background.

docs.title: Pitfall 2: Red and Green Without Transition
docs.text: Red and green are complementary, and slamming them together in large blocks without a buffer creates an unrefined, dated look. If you must use both, separate them with white, beige, or grey.

docs.title: Pitfall 3: More Than 4 Colors Without Hierarchy
docs.text:
A cover using 5 or 6 colors in roughly equal proportions looks chaotic and unfocused.

Color Count Guideline

Stick to 3-4 colors maximum per design, with a clear primary-secondary hierarchy. If you must use more, let 1-2 colors dominate.

docs.title: Pitfall 4: Low Contrast Between Text and Background
docs.text:
Light grey text on a white background, dark blue text on a black background — when text and background are too similar, readability tanks.

Quick test: Turn your phone brightness to minimum. If you can't read the text, your contrast is too low.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Rule Core Point Difficulty 60-30-10 Dominant 60%, secondary 30%, accent 10% Easy Monochromatic Same hue, varying brightness/saturation Easy Complementary Opposite colors, mind the ratio and buffers Medium Pitfall Checklist Avoid neon, raw contrast, too many colors, low contrast —

Next Step

Rules mastered — now you need the right tools to put them into practice. The next chapter, 2.4 Tools & Practice, recommends apps and online platforms to take you from theory to execution.

docs.title: 2.2 Style Palettes
docs.text: Theory covered — now for the most practical part: curated palettes organized by style, complete with hex codes, ready to drop into your covers, backgrounds, and text. Find the group that matches your content vibe and start using it today.

docs.title: Morandi Tones
docs.text:
The essence of Morandi colors: every hue looks like it's been filtered through a layer of soft grey. Low saturation, moderate brightness — gentle, sophisticated, and never loud. This is the quintessential "elevated" palette on Xiaohongshu.

#8EA4B5 Dusty Blue #C4A882 Rose Beige #A2B5A0 Sage Green #C8B49A Milk Tea

Pairing Advice

Morandi tones are nearly impossible to clash with each other. Use a lighter shade for the background, a deeper tone for text, and pick any of the others as an accent.

Best for: Home decor, OOTD, lifestyle, cafe reviews

Pairing notes:

  • Choose the lightest tone (like Milk Tea) for backgrounds; use dark grey or dark brown for text
  • Avoid pairing with high-saturation colors — they'll break the muted harmony
  • Add generous white space to keep the layout breathable
My Slow Weekend

coffee · books · sunlight

This Morandi palette is so soothing | color params included Lifestyle Curator ♡ 2.3k

docs.title: High Saturation Contrast
docs.text: The polar opposite of Morandi — high-saturation contrast is all about bold visual impact. Two vivid, opposing colors collide to grab attention instantly. Perfect for content that needs to pop off the screen.

docs.title: Combo 1: Klein Blue + Bright Orange
docs.text: #002FA7 Klein Blue #FF6B35 Bright Orange

docs.title: Combo 2: True Red + Off-White
docs.text: #E03C31 True Red #F5F0E8 Off-White

docs.title: Combo 3: Purple + YellowToday's Look
docs.text:
#7B68EE Purple #FFD700 Yellow

Contrast Pitfall

Contrast doesn't mean "split the canvas 50/50 with two loud colors." One color should dominate (60-70%), while the other serves as an accent (30-40%). Without this hierarchy, the result is chaotic, not striking.

Best for: Beauty tutorials, food reviews, streetwear, event promotions

Pairing notes:

  • Let one color fill the majority of the canvas; use the other for titles or localized accents
  • White or light grey can serve as a buffer zone to ease visual tension
  • High-contrast palettes work better on image covers than long-form text layouts

Klein Blue × Sunset Orange

This color clash is stunning! A must-try fall look Beauty Girl ♡ 5.1k

docs.title: Cream Tones
docs.text:
The keywords here: soft, warm, pillowy, comforting. Every color looks like it's been run through a cream filter — deeply soothing. This is the go-to palette for Korean- and Japanese-style creators.

#FAF6F0 Cream White #F7E8C8 Pale Yellow #F2D7C9 Light Apricot #D6E4EC Pale Blue Grey

Typical Application

When using cream tones for a cover, set your text in dark brown (e.g., #5C4033) or dark slate (e.g., #4A5568) instead of pure black. Pure black is too harsh and breaks the softness.

Best for: Japanese-style OOTD, Korean cafe reviews, healing vlogs, journal sharing, dessert baking

Pairing notes:

  • Keep the overall palette in the high-brightness, low-saturation range
  • These tones mix and match freely without clashing
  • Use dark brown or grey tones for text — avoid pure black
  • Especially well-suited for vertical covers and maintaining a unified grid aesthetic
Afternoon Tea for One

☁️ soft · warm · cozy

Creamy room makeover | cozy on a budget Cream Girl ♡ 8.7k

docs.title: Dark & Gold
docs.text:
Dark & gold communicates: quality, power, professionalism. A deep background with gold accents naturally conveys premium status. The go-to palette for tech, business, and masculine-coded content.

#1A1A1A Rich Black #C9A96E Gold #333333 Charcoal

Why Does Black & Gold Feel Luxurious?

Black represents mystery and power; gold symbolizes wealth and quality. Together, they create strong light-dark contrast, while gold's inherent warmth tempers black's coldness — resulting in a look that's both powerful and approachable.

Best for: Tech gadgets, business insights, men's fashion, luxury unboxing, finance content

Pairing notes:

  • Use #1A1A1A as your background — it has more texture than pure #000000
  • Reserve gold for titles, icons, dividers, and other small-area elements
  • Charcoal serves as a transition tone to prevent the layout from feeling too binary
  • Set body text in white or light grey to ensure readability
2024 Best Picks

TOP 10 · Worth every repurchase

Dark & gold cover tutorial | premium look in 3 steps Tech Reviewer ♡ 3.4k

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Palette Keywords Saturation Content Fit Morandi Sophisticated, muted, serene Low Home, OOTD, lifestyle High Contrast Bold, vibrant, attention-grabbing High Beauty, food, streetwear Cream Soft, healing, warm Low-Medium J-style, K-style, desserts Dark & Gold Premium, powerful, professional Low-Medium Tech, business, men's content

Next Step

Palette chosen — now what? How do you allocate these colors across your cover? The next chapter, 2.3 Matching Rules, gives you concrete ratios and methods.

docs.title: 2.1 Color Theory
docs.text: On Xiaohongshu, color is often the very first impression a user gets from your post. Before you start picking hex codes, spend a few minutes understanding the fundamentals — this knowledge will serve you far better than memorizing any single palette.

docs.title: The Color Wheel
docs.text:
The color wheel is the foundational tool for understanding how colors relate to each other.

Primary Colors

At the core of the color wheel are three primary colors: Red, Yellow, and Blue. Every other color is derived from mixing these three.

Secondary colors are created by mixing two primaries in equal amounts:

Mixture Secondary Color Red + Yellow Orange Yellow + Blue Green Red + Blue Purple

Complementary colors sit directly opposite each other on the wheel — red & green, blue & orange, yellow & purple. Placed together, they create strong visual contrast. Used skillfully, this creates a striking, high-end look. Used carelessly, it looks garish.

A Rule to Remember

The closer two colors are on the wheel, the more harmonious they feel. The farther apart, the stronger the contrast. Before picking colors, ask yourself: do I want harmony or impact?

The rich world of color — understanding hue, saturation, and brightness is the foundation of any palette

docs.title: The HSB Model
docs.text:
Rather than memorizing specific hex codes (like #8EA4B5), understanding three dimensions of color will help you much more.

H — Hue

Hue is what we colloquially mean by "color" — red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple. It corresponds to a position on the color wheel, measured from 0° to 360°.

S — Saturation

Saturation controls how vivid or muted a color appears. Higher saturation means more vivid and intense; lower saturation means greyer and softer.

Saturation Effect Good For High (80-100%) Vibrant, energetic, eye-catching Beauty, food, fashion-forward Medium (40-70%) Comfortable, natural OOTD, travel, home decor Low (10-30%) Sophisticated, quiet, grey-toned Morandi, wabi-sabi

B — Brightness

Brightness controls how light or dark a color is. High brightness leans toward white; low brightness leans toward black.

In Practice

Take blue: high saturation + high brightness = Klein Blue (bold and energetic), while low saturation + medium brightness = dusty blue (soft and serene). Once you understand HSB, you can derive an entire palette from a single starting color.

docs.title: Color Temperature
docs.text:
Colors have a perceived "temperature" that directly shapes the mood of your image.

Warm tones: Red, Orange, Yellow

  • Evoke warmth, passion, energy, and approachability
  • Great for food, outfit, home decor, and lifestyle content
  • Psychologically close the distance between you and the viewer

Cool tones: Blue, Green, Purple

  • Evoke calm, rationality, freshness, and sophistication
  • Great for tech, business, travel, and minimalist content
  • Create a sense of space and detachment

Xiaohongshu Trend

Mixing warm and cool tones has been hugely popular on the platform recently — think warm-toned imagery with cool-toned text, or a cool filter with warm accent touches. This blend creates images that feel both inviting and elevated.

Warm tones — warmth and energy Cool tones — calm and sophisticated

docs.title: Color Psychology
docs.text:
Every color unconsciously triggers specific emotions and associations. Understanding these effects lets you use color to communicate before a single word is read.

Color Psychological Association Content Fit Red Passion, energy, urgency, festivity Sales, beauty, holidays Blue Calm, trust, professionalism, breadth Tech, business, travel Green Nature, health, freshness, relaxation Fitness, food, outdoors Pink Softness, sweetness, romance Beauty, OOTD, dating Yellow Joy, liveliness, attention-grabbing Food, parenting, study tips Black Luxury, mystery, power Tech, luxury goods, men's content White Clean, minimal, breathing room Minimalist, Japanese style, medical beauty

A Caveat

Color psychology is not absolute. The same hue at different saturation, brightness, and in different combinations can convey entirely different moods. A bright red screams passion; a dark burgundy might feel somber. Always consider context.

docs.title: Takeaway
docs.text: Understand the color wheel, master the three HSB dimensions, sense temperature differences, and learn color psychology — with these fundamentals, you're no longer picking colors by gut feeling. You're choosing them with intention. Next up, we'll hand you curated palettes organized by style, ready to use.

docs.title: 2.4 Tools & Practice
docs.text: You've got the theory and the rules — the final step is putting them to work in your actual content. This chapter recommends practical color tools and walks you through a complete start-to-finish workflow.

docs.title: Xingtu (醒图)
docs.text:
Xingtu is one of the most popular photo editing apps among Xiaohongshu creators, packed with filters and color adjustment features.

Xingtu Color Tips

  • The HSL panel lets you adjust saturation and brightness for individual hues, giving you precise control over your image's tone
  • Built-in filters can unify your entire image's color palette in one tap
  • The "Templates" feature offers ready-made color schemes — great for beginners

Best for: Photo color grading, filter-based tone unification, quick edits

docs.title: Canva
docs.text:
Canva is an online design platform whose biggest advantage is that templates come with built-in color schemes.

  • Search "Xiaohongshu" to find templates pre-sized for the platform
  • Every template has a preset palette — click any color block to see the full scheme
  • Supports custom color replacement — find a template with colors you like, swap in your own content

Canva's Hidden Feature

Upload any image you love, and Canva will automatically extract its dominant colors into a palette. This is a fantastic way to discover color inspiration.

Best for: Cover design, palette reference from templates, quick layouts

docs.title: Coolors
docs.text:
Coolors is a professional online palette generator.

  • Press the spacebar to randomly generate a 5-color palette
  • Lock colors you like and regenerate only the others
  • Supports extracting palettes from uploaded images
  • Includes a colorblind accessibility checker

Best for: Finding palette inspiration, exploring new combinations, professional color work

docs.title: Adobe Color
docs.text:
Adobe Color is Adobe's powerful color tool.

  • Interactive color wheel — select rules like "complementary," "analogous," or "triadic" to auto-generate palettes
  • The "Explore" page showcases trending color themes
  • Supports palette extraction from images
  • Checks color contrast against accessibility standards

When to Use Adobe Color

When you've settled on a primary color but aren't sure what to pair it with, input it into Adobe Color and select "complementary" or "analogous." The tool will generate a harmonious palette for you automatically.

Best for: Systematic wheel-based palettes, contrast checking, professional design

docs.title: Butter Camera (黄油相机)
docs.text:
Butter Camera's strength is typography and text overlays, with solid color features as well.

  • Extensive font template library, each with recommended color pairings
  • Stickers and filters help unify the overall image tone
  • Supports custom text colors with direct hex code input

Best for: Text layout, title overlays on covers, text color consistency

docs.title: Step-by-Step Workflow
docs.text: Tools in hand, here's a complete workflow for color matching. Follow these 5 steps every time you design a cover:

docs.title: Step 1: Define Your Style
docs.text:
Ask yourself: what mood should this post convey?

Target Mood Recommended Palette Sophisticated, serene Morandi tones Vibrant, eye-catching High-saturation contrast Soft, comforting Cream tones Professional, premium Dark & Gold

docs.title: Step 2: Choose Your Dominant Color
docs.text: From the corresponding palette, pick one dominant color (60%). This will be your background or base color.

docs.title: Step 3: Pick a Secondary Color
docs.text: From the same palette, select a secondary color (30%) for supporting elements — color blocks, decorations, subheadings.

docs.title: Step 4: Set Your Accent
docs.text: Choose a color with some contrast to your dominant shade as the accent (10%). Use it for titles, buttons, and key highlights.

docs.title: Step 5: Unify with a Filter
docs.text:
Finally, apply a cohesive filter in Xingtu or Butter Camera to bring all your colors into the same tonal family.

A Common Mistake

Many creators nail the first four steps but skip the fifth. Without a unifying filter, even perfectly chosen hex codes can clash when photos and text don't share the same tonal quality.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Tool Core Strength Price Xingtu Photo color grading, filters Free / Premium Canva Template palettes, design layout Free / Pro Coolors Random palette generation Free / Pro Adobe Color Wheel-based palettes, contrast checks Free Butter Camera Typography, text color styling Free / Premium

Final Advice

Tools are aids; taste is what matters. Spend time studying high-engagement posts on Xiaohongshu — analyze which colors they use, in what proportions, and in what style. The more you observe, the sharper your color instinct becomes.

docs.title: 3.3 Multi-Image Arrangement — Tell a Complete Story with Your Photos
docs.text: Xiaohongshu posts can contain up to 18 images. But more images do not automatically mean better — what matters is the role each image plays and where it sits in the sequence. A well-arranged post is like a story: it has a beginning, a middle, and an end.

docs.title: Image Sequence Logic: Every Position Has a Purpose
docs.text: Users pay different levels of attention to images depending on their position. Understanding each slot's role lets you maximize the value of every photo.

docs.title: Image 1 (Cover): The Make-or-Break Shot
docs.text:
The cover is the only image that appears in the feed. It determines whether users tap into your post at all.

How Critical Is the Cover?

The cover image accounts for over 50% of a post's success. You should spend more time on your cover than on all other images combined.

The cover has one job: make people want to tap in. It needs to —

  • Feature a prominent subject with instantly clear information
  • Have strong visual impact that stands out in the feed
  • Work together with the title to convey the core message
Img 1 Cover Img 2 Core Img 3 Detail Img 4 Mood

docs.title: Images 2-3: Core Content Delivery
docs.text:
Once a user taps into the post, these are the first images they examine closely. They need to carry the post's primary value.

How to Plan Images 2-3 by Category

  • Tutorials: Show the key steps or core techniques
  • Outfit posts: Different angles — full-body shots and detail close-ups
  • Reviews: Real product shots and usage results
  • Guides: The most important information or actionable takeaways

docs.title: Images 4-6: Details and Supplementary Information
docs.text:
By this point, the user already understands your core message. Images 4-6 provide depth through details, comparisons, and supporting information.

Content that works well in these slots:

  • Product detail close-ups
  • Before-and-after comparisons
  • The product or subject shown in different settings
  • Informational graphics with supplementary details

docs.title: Images 7+: Wrap-Up and Engagement
docs.text:
Users who reach this far are already highly interested. This is the prime spot for driving engagement.

Smart Uses for Later Images

  • A summary infographic (key takeaways from the post)
  • Product lists or purchasing information
  • Engagement prompts ("Which one do you prefer? Tell me in the comments!")
  • Related recommendations or series previews

docs.title: Visual Consistency Across Images
docs.text: Arrangement is not just about content sequence — it is also about visual rhythm.

docs.title: Unified Color Tone
docs.text: This was emphasized in the previous chapter, and it is equally important here. Swiping from image 1 to image 9, the color palette should feel continuous. A sudden style shift mid-post is jarring.

docs.title: Unified Style
docs.text:
Within a single post, shooting angles, composition approach, and editing style should all remain consistent.

A Cautionary Example

Image 1 is a carefully styled product shot, image 2 is a blurry casual screenshot, image 3 is a graphic downloaded from the internet — this kind of mismatch causes the perceived quality of the entire post to plummet.

docs.title: Visual Rhythm
docs.text:
Good arrangement has rhythm. Wide shots and close-ups alternate, information density varies between dense and light, colors echo and evolve. Like a piece of music, you cannot play the same note from start to finish.

A Simple Rhythm Formula

Wide shot (establish context) → Medium shot (show the subject) → Close-up (highlight details) → Wide shot (new angle or scene) → Repeat

docs.title: Portrait vs. Landscape vs. Square
docs.text:
The aspect ratio you choose directly affects how your images are displayed.

Ratio Dimensions Advantage Best For Portrait 3:4 1080x1440 Largest footprint in the feed, strongest visual impact The default choice for most posts Square 1:1 1080x1080 Clean, grid-friendly layout Information-heavy posts, product grids Landscape 16:9 1920x1080 Wide field of view, great for expansive scenes Scenery, side-by-side comparisons, before/after

The Best Default

Portrait 3:4 is Xiaohongshu's golden ratio. It occupies the most screen real estate in the feed and holds user attention the longest. Unless you have a specific reason to choose otherwise, go with portrait.

One important reminder: All images within a single post must use the same aspect ratio. Mixing portrait and landscape images causes the system to auto-crop, which can cut off critical parts of your photos.

The Cost of Mixed Ratios

Always preview your post before publishing. If you notice that key information has been cropped out, adjust the ratio and re-upload.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
• The cover is the top priority — it determines whether your post lives or dies
  • The first three images carry the core value; later images supplement and drive engagement
  • Visual consistency matters more than any single image being perfect
  • Portrait 3:4 is the optimal default — keep all images in a post at the same ratio

In the next chapter, we will look at specific cover image types — which formats work best for different content categories.

docs.title: 3.2 Editing & Filters — Unify Your Style, Elevate Your Quality
docs.text: Selecting great images is only the first step. Through post-processing and color grading, you can take your visuals to the next level. But editing is a double-edged sword — done well, it enhances; overdone, it detracts.

docs.title: The Core Principle of Color Grading: Consistency
docs.text:
The single most important rule of photo editing is: consistency.

Every image within a single post must maintain a unified color tone. If image 1 is warm yellow, image 2 is cool blue, image 3 is highly saturated, and image 4 is black and white — that mismatch makes the entire post feel chaotic and significantly hurts the reading experience.

How to Achieve Consistency

  • Apply the same filter or the same set of grading parameters to all images in a post
  • If shooting conditions varied, first unify white balance and exposure before applying filters
  • Save your frequently used presets to build a signature style

Consistent color grading does not just make individual posts look better — it gives your entire profile a cohesive visual identity. When users visit your profile and see a unified aesthetic, they are far more likely to follow.

docs.title: Popular Filter Styles
docs.text: Here are the most popular filter aesthetics on Xiaohongshu, each suited to different content types:

docs.title: Japanese Style
docs.text:
Characteristics: High brightness, low saturation, warm tones, soft overall feel

Best for: Daily life, food, literary fashion, cafe reviews

Grading Direction

Slightly increase exposure, reduce contrast, lower saturation, push color temperature warm, shift highlights toward yellow, lift shadows. The goal is a "soft and warm" feeling throughout.

docs.title: Korean Style
docs.text:
Characteristics: Cool tones, clean and transparent feel, low saturation, crisp and polished

Best for: Skincare, minimalist fashion, minimal home decor, atmospheric content

Grading Direction

Push color temperature cool, lower saturation, moderate contrast, shift highlights toward blue-white, keep skin tones natural. Aim for a "cool, elevated" aesthetic.

docs.title: Vintage Style
docs.text:
Characteristics: Warm yellow tones, low contrast, slight film grain

Best for: Travel, street photography, film-inspired daily life, vintage fashion

Grading Direction

Push color temperature warm, shift shadows toward yellow-green, shift highlights toward orange, lower contrast, add a touch of grain. Emulate the look of analog film.

Japanese — high brightness + low saturation Vintage — warm yellow + low contrast

docs.title: Muted Luxury Style
docs.text:
Characteristics: Low saturation, high perceived quality, restrained color palette, overall gray tonality

Best for: Premium products, minimalist aesthetics, architecture photography, elevated fashion

Grading Direction

Significantly lower saturation, keep contrast moderate to slightly high, push the overall tone toward gray, retain a hint of the dominant color. Pursue the restrained beauty of "less is more."

docs.title: Recommended Editing Tools
docs.text:
Different tools suit different needs and skill levels:

Tool Strengths Best For Platform Xingtu Feature-rich, abundant templates, Chinese-friendly interface Beginners to intermediate iOS / Android VSCO Exceptionally high-quality filters, refined color aesthetics Users who prioritize quality iOS / Android Lightroom Professional-grade grading, precise parameter control Intermediate to professional iOS / Android / Desktop Snapseed Made by Google, excellent local adjustment tools Users who need detailed retouching iOS / Android

Beginner Recommendation

If you are just starting out, begin with Xingtu. Its filter templates can be applied with one tap, and you can manually fine-tune the parameters. Once you are comfortable with grading logic, move on to VSCO or Lightroom.

docs.title: Editing Pitfalls: Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text: The biggest risk in editing is trying too hard. Here are the most common ways things go wrong:

docs.title: Over-Smoothing Skin
docs.text:
The Plastic Face Problem

Smoothing skin until the nose disappears and the texture looks like plastic — this kind of editing is not only unattractive but also erodes trust. Retain some natural skin texture for a result that looks both real and beautiful.

docs.title: Inconsistent Filters
docs.text: Using different styles across images in the same post is a cardinal sin. Even if each image looks fine individually, clashing styles destroy the overall visual coherence when viewed together.

docs.title: Brightness Too High or Too Low
docs.text: Overexposure washes out the image and loses detail; underexposure makes content hard to see. After editing, try lowering your screen brightness and checking again — many people keep their phone brightness permanently high, which causes their edited images to appear too dark on other devices.

docs.title: Over-Sharpening
docs.text:
Moderate sharpening can enhance clarity, but overdoing it creates unnatural white edges and noise. The image ends up looking "harsh" and "artificial."

The Highest Level of Editing

When someone thinks "this photo looks amazing" but cannot pinpoint what was edited — that is the gold standard. Great post-processing is invisible. It elevates quality without leaving a trace.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
• Rule number one of color grading: Consistency, consistency, consistency
  • Find the filter style that fits you: Japanese, Korean, vintage, and muted luxury each have their strengths
  • Tools — quality over quantity: Mastering one tool is better than dabbling in ten
  • Exercise restraint: Over-editing is worse than not editing at all

In the next chapter, we will discuss multi-image arrangement — once your images are selected and edited, the order in which you present them matters more than you might think.

docs.title: Part 3: Imagery — The Visual Code That Makes People Click
docs.text: Great content paired with great images is what truly gets seen. This part helps you master image selection, editing, arrangement, and cover design.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
On Xiaohongshu, images are the primary currency. Whether a post gets clicked depends 80% on the cover image; whether it gets read through depends on the quality and arrangement of every single photo. Choosing images is not a casual afterthought — it is a complete visual strategy.

This part will systematically help you build an imagery mindset, covering selection criteria, editing and filters, multi-image arrangement, and cover image types.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
• What makes an image worth selecting
  • How to unify visual style through editing and filters
  • The logic and rhythm of arranging multiple images
  • Which cover image types work best for different content categories

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text: # Chapter Key Topics 3.1 Image Selection Principles Subject prominence, clean backgrounds, lighting, sharpness, emotional storytelling 3.2 Editing & Filters Color grading principles, filter styles, recommended tools, common mistakes 3.3 Multi-Image Arrangement Image sequence logic, visual consistency, portrait vs. landscape orientation 3.4 Cover Image Types Person-featured, product flat lay, comparison, text poster, scene & atmosphere

docs.title: Suggested Learning Path
docs.text:
Work through the chapters in order: first master the fundamentals of image selection, then learn to unify your style with color grading, understand the rhythm of multi-image arrangement, and finally choose the right cover type for your content category.

After each chapter, practice with your own images — hands-on experimentation is far more effective than reading alone.

docs.title: 3.1 Image Selection Principles — Good Images Speak for Themselves
docs.text: On Xiaohongshu, a single great image outweighs a thousand words of copy. Selecting images is not as simple as "picking something pretty" — there are clear, actionable standards. Master these five core principles and your image quality will improve dramatically.

docs.title: Subject Prominence: Make It Instantly Clear
docs.text:
The subject is the soul of your image. In a proper Xiaohongshu photo, the subject should occupy at least 60% of the frame.

Users spend no more than 1-2 seconds on each image while scrolling. If the subject is not prominent enough, they will swipe past before even registering what you are sharing.

Practical Advice

Get closer to your subject when shooting, or crop aggressively during editing. A tight composition is always better than a subject that is too small.

Common mistakes:

  • In outfit posts, the person occupies only one-third of the frame, surrounded by cluttered background
  • Product shots taken from too far away, missing important details
  • Food photos where the plate sits in a tiny corner

Quick test: After taking a photo, shrink it to thumbnail size on your phone. Can you still clearly identify the subject? If not, re-crop or re-shoot.

✓ Clear subject, filling 60%+ of the frame ✓ Close-up framing, full of detail

docs.title: Clean Backgrounds: Supporting, Not Competing
docs.text:
The background's job is to complement the subject, not compete with it for attention.

Three Types of Great Backgrounds

  • Solid colors: White walls, plain tabletops, solid fabric — clean and versatile
  • Blurred backgrounds: Use portrait mode or a wide aperture to naturally isolate the subject
  • Simple scenes: A tidy desk, a clean street, an organized space

Cluttered backgrounds are the most common beginner mistake. Objects piled on a desk, strangers in the background, posters on the wall — all of these pull attention away from what matters.

Save Yourself Time

Spending 30 seconds tidying the background before shooting saves far more time than fixing it in post-production. Build the habit of "tidy first, shoot second."

docs.title: Lighting: Natural Light Is the Best Filter
docs.text:
Lighting directly determines the quality and feel of your image. The same subject can look completely different under different lighting conditions.

Three commonly used lighting directions:

Lighting Type Effect Best For Front light Bright, clear image with accurate colors Product shots, full-body outfit photos Side light Creates depth and dimension with highlights and shadows Portraits, food, texture showcase Backlight Strong atmosphere, creates a halo or glow effect Lifestyle vlogs, mood-driven scenes

Golden Shooting Hours

Natural light is softest between 9-11 AM and 3-5 PM. Midday sun is too harsh and creates heavy shadows. Overcast days act as a natural softbox — perfect for shooting.

When shooting indoors, position yourself near a window to take advantage of natural light. If light is insufficient, use a white card as a reflector — this produces much better results than a flash.

Front light — bright and clear Side light — depth and dimension Backlight — strong atmosphere

docs.title: Sharpness: Blurry Images Are a Deal-Breaker
docs.text:
This seems like the most basic point, yet it is overlooked with surprising frequency.

Blurry images give the impression of carelessness and lack of effort. This is especially critical for cover images — a blurry cover is essentially telling users to keep scrolling.

Common causes of blur:

  • Camera shake: not holding the phone steady
  • Mis-focus: the focal point lands on the background instead of the subject
  • Low light: the camera compensates with a slower shutter speed
  • Over-cropping: zooming in too much degrades image quality

Tips for Staying Sharp

  • Hold your phone with both hands, elbows tucked against your body
  • Tap the subject on screen to manually set focus
  • In low light, use a tripod or rest your phone on a stable surface
  • Take burst shots and pick the sharpest one

docs.title: Emotional Storytelling: Great Images Have a Narrative
docs.text:
Once the technical boxes are checked, the final dimension is emotion.

Truly compelling images are not just sharp and attractive — they create a sense of immersion. The viewer sees a photo and thinks, "I want to visit that place," "I want to own that thing," or "I want to live that kind of life."

Techniques for Emotional Impact

  • Include human elements: A hand holding a coffee cup, a person's silhouette, an open book on a table — traces of human presence add warmth
  • Capture motion: The moment coffee is poured, a page being turned, hair caught by the wind — movement feels more alive than static poses
  • Use light and color: Warm afternoon sun, raindrops on a window, golden sunset tones — light and shadow are emotions in themselves

Do not just aim for "pretty." Aim for "feeling." Technique is the foundation, but emotion is what elevates an image from adequate to outstanding.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
The five core principles of image selection, ranked by importance:

  1. Sharpness — The baseline; a blurry image disqualifies everything else
  2. Subject prominence — Make the content instantly recognizable
  3. Clean background — Eliminate distractions
  4. Lighting — The key to elevating quality
  5. Emotional storytelling — The difference between good and great

In the next chapter, we will cover editing and filters — how to take a well-selected image and elevate it further through post-processing.

docs.title: 3.4 Cover Image Types — Pick the Right Format and Double Your Click-Through Rate
docs.text: Different content categories call for different cover formats. Choosing the right type boosts click-through rate far more than just improving image quality.

docs.title: Five Major Cover Types at a Glance
docs.text: Type Characteristics Best Categories Click Power Person-featured Clear face, expressive emotion Outfits, beauty, vlogs High Product flat lay Items neatly arranged, shot from above Hauls, unboxing, favorites Medium-high Comparison Side-by-side or top-bottom before/after Tutorials, reviews, makeovers High Text poster Bold text, information-first design Tips, guides, lists High Scene & atmosphere Lifestyle setting, immersive feel Home decor, travel, food Medium 👩Person Today's OOTD  Style Blogger 📦Flat Lay Unboxing | These finds are amazing  Haul Queen BeforeAfter Room Makeover Comparison  Makeover Diary 5 Tips10x better covers Text Poster Cover Tutorial  Design Pro

docs.title: Person-Featured: The Most Compelling Cover Type
docs.text:
今日穿搭 OOTD ✨ 穿搭博主 ♡ 4.2k

Humans are naturally drawn to faces. Covers featuring a person grab attention more easily in the feed. Keep the face clear, expressions compelling, and the background simple.

When to Use

Outfit shares, beauty tutorials, lifestyle vlogs, fitness check-ins.

Watch Out

Avoid over-filtering to the point of looking unreal. Users increasingly value authenticity.

docs.title: Product Flat Lay: The Classic Choice for Hauls
docs.text:
近期爱用好物合集｜平价也有好质感 好物分享家 ♡ 6.8k

Arrange products neatly on a surface and shoot from directly above. Its strength is information density — one image showcases multiple products. Keep arrangement orderly, spacing breathable, and backgrounds clean.

Advanced Technique

Small decorative elements (dried flowers, a magazine, a coffee cup) add depth, but should never overshadow the products.

docs.title: Comparison: Naturally Attention-Grabbing
docs.text:
Before After

People are instinctively curious about change. Showing "before" and "after" in a single image creates powerful visual impact. Make the contrast obvious, keep shooting conditions consistent, and label it clearly.

When to Use

Makeup tutorials, skincare reviews, room makeovers, outfit comparisons, organization transformations.

Credibility Is Everything

Do not exaggerate results by manipulating lighting or angles. If users suspect dishonesty, the backlash will be severe.

docs.title: Text Poster: The Information-First Powerhouse
docs.text:
5 Color Tips Double your post aesthetics

Put the core message directly on the cover so users see the topic and value while still in the feed. Text must be large and legible at thumbnail size, messaging concise, and colors harmonious.

When to Use

Tips and tutorials, guide compilations, knowledge sharing, warning lists. Especially effective for "save-worthy" content.

docs.title: Scene & Atmosphere: Drawing Viewers In
docs.text:
周末的家｜阳光洒进来的样子 家居日记 ♡ 9.1k

Atmospheric covers aim for immersion — making the viewer think "I want to live like that." Use natural light, warm tones, and lifestyle details to set the mood.

Quick Atmosphere Boost

Early morning and golden hour light naturally carry mood. Indoors, turn off overhead lights and use a desk lamp or window light.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Cover Type for You
docs.text:
Consider three factors: content category (match to use cases above), competition (see what top-performing posts use), and personal strengths (comfortable on camera? use person-featured; strong design skills? lean into text posters).

The Ultimate Advice

Do not limit yourself to one cover type — switch flexibly based on content. But within a category, keep styles consistent for a polished profile.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
• Person-featured and comparison covers deliver the highest click-through rates but demand the best source material
  • Text posters are powerful for tips-and-guides content
  • Product flat lays and atmospheric scenes rely on aesthetic sense and composition skills
  • Weigh your content category, competitor benchmarks, and personal strengths when choosing

docs.title: 4.2 Body Text Rhythm
docs.text:
The title gets the tap; the body decides whether the reader stays. A well-written post reads like music — it has rhythm, breathing room, and flow. If your text is a dense wall of words, even the best content will make people scroll away.

✗ Wall of text Went to a coffee shop today Today my friend and I went to that new coffee shop the one that went viral online we ordered a latte and an americano and a tiramisu cake the taste was pretty good and the vibe was nice too just a bit crowded had to wait in line for about twenty minutes but I really liked the decor it has that vintage feel and its really photogenic I recommend everyone go check it out the price is not bad either about fifty per person so overall definitely worth a visit next time I want to try their other desserts too ✓ Rhythm and breathing room Hidden gem cafe in the alley ☕ Finally visited this spot everyone raves about. ✨ Vibe Vintage French decor — every corner is photo-worthy. ☕ Must-order · Oat latte (a must!)· Tiramisu· Basque cheesecake 📍 Details ~$7/person · 322 Anfu RoadGo early on weekends — ~20min wait

docs.title: Paragraph Rhythm: Alternating Short and Long Sentences
docs.text:
Good body-text rhythm works like breathing — short sentences are the exhale, punchy and energetic; long sentences are the inhale, expansive and explanatory. Alternating between the two keeps reading effortless.

Compare

No rhythm: "Today I want to share some cover design tips I have been researching for a long time actually cover design is not as hard as you might think you just need to know a few core principles and you will be able to make great covers."

With rhythm: "Is cover design hard? Not really. Master a few core principles and even a total beginner can create polished covers. Today I am breaking down the techniques I have tested over months — simple, practical, and ready to use."

Pro Tip

  • Keep sentences under roughly 20 words for the easiest reading experience
  • Use short sentences for key points; use longer ones for explanations
  • Break to a new line every 2-3 sentences to prevent visual fatigue

docs.title: Section Dividers: Visual Punctuation for Your Text
docs.text: Xiaohongshu posts are closer to "visual notes" than traditional articles. The right divider style makes your information hierarchy instantly clear.

docs.title: Option A: Emoji Dividers
docs.text:
Use emoji as the visual anchor for each paragraph. Colorful and friendly — ideal for lifestyle and product-recommendation posts.

Sample

✨ Step 1: Pick your main color

🎨 Step 2: Add 1-2 supporting colors

💡 Step 3: Use a neutral tone for the background

Best For

Product roundups, tutorials, everyday sharing. Keep your emoji style consistent — do not mix flowers, stars, and animals randomly.

docs.title: Option B: Blank-Line Dividers
docs.text:
The cleanest approach: simply separate information blocks with empty lines.

Sample

Today I am sharing 3 cover layout tips.

First: do not overload the text. One or two lines of key words on the cover is enough.

Second: white space matters. Do not cram elements edge to edge.

Third: alignment. Keep all text and images aligned in a consistent way.

docs.title: Option C: Numbered Lists
docs.text:
Perfect for how-to content and step-by-step guides — the logical structure helps readers locate information fast.

Sample

  1. Choose a template
  2. Swap in your own images and text
  3. Adjust colors to match your style
  4. Export and publish

Which One to Pick

  • Lifestyle or product posts → emoji dividers
  • Opinion or emotional posts → blank-line dividers
  • Tutorials or how-to posts → numbered lists
  • Stick to one style per post for a unified look

docs.title: Information Density: One Point per Paragraph
docs.text:
This is the most common beginner mistake — cramming three or four ideas into a single paragraph. The result is exhausting to read and impossible to remember.

Avoid This

Overloaded paragraph: "For covers, pay attention to colors, do not use too much text, leave some white space, also font choice matters — I recommend Source Han Sans or Zhanku fonts — and for layout you should center-align everything."

One point per paragraph: "Cover colors: 2-3 colors are plenty. Too many make it look chaotic." (new paragraph) "Keep text minimal: only 1-2 lines of key info on the cover. More than that and nothing stands out."

The Sticky-Note Rule

Think of each paragraph as a sticky note — one note, one idea. If you find yourself chaining points together with "also," "and," or "by the way," it is time to split the paragraph.

docs.title: Visual Breathing Room: White Space Is the Secret to Polish
docs.text:
"Breathing room" is the soul of Xiaohongshu layout. It means leaving enough space between lines and paragraphs so the reader's eyes can rest.

✗ Text Wall ✓ With Rhythm ✨

Text Wall vs. Breathing Room

  • Text wall: Long paragraphs, no blank lines, emoji overload, dense information → readers bounce immediately
  • Breathing room: Short paragraphs, blank lines between sections, clear information points, visual comfort → readers stay to the end

Quick Rules

  • No paragraph longer than 3-4 lines
  • One blank line between paragraphs
  • Important points get their own standalone paragraph
  • Never use more than 3 emoji in a row

docs.title: The Opening: Your First Two Lines Are Make-or-Break
docs.text:
After tapping in, the reader's eyes land on the first two lines of your body text. If those lines fail to hook their attention, they leave.

High-Impact Openers

  • Create empathy: "Do you agonize over your cover every single time? I used to, too..."
  • Spark curiosity: "After I started using this method, my saves tripled."
  • Lead with value: "Today I am sharing 5 layout tricks I have used for six months — every one of them tested and proven."

Openings to Avoid

  • "Hi everyone, today I want to share..." — too flat, zero information
  • "A lot of people have been asking me..." — unless you are a major creator, readers do not care

docs.title: The Closing: Guide the Interaction
docs.text:
Your ending is not just "the last sentence" — it is your final chance to prompt the reader to take action.

Interaction Prompts

  • Ask a question: "What app do you use for covers? Tell me in the comments!"
  • Create a poll: "Do you prefer Option A or Option B?"
  • Encourage saves: "If this was helpful, save it for next time you design a cover!"
  • Tease the next post: "Next up: color palette tips — follow me so you do not miss it!"

docs.title: Chapter Recap
docs.text:
Body text layout is not something you "just write and post" — it deserves the same care as designing a cover. Remember: rhythm, dividers, density, breathing room, opening, and closing — every element shapes whether readers finish your post or scroll away.

Practice Exercise

Find a viral post that feels effortless to read, then analyze it paragraph by paragraph: What divider style does it use? How many lines per paragraph? How does it open? How does it prompt interaction at the end?

Next chapter: 4.3 Elegant Keyword Embedding — make your posts discoverable through search.

docs.title: Part 4: Copywriting Aesthetics
docs.text: A beautiful cover gets the click, but beautiful copy keeps the reader. Copywriting on Xiaohongshu is more than just words — it is a blend of visual rhythm, information design, and search strategy. This part teaches you how to craft titles, body text, and keywords that are both pleasing to read and powerful in reach.

docs.title: 4.1 The Art of Titles
docs.text: The title is the very first sentence a user reads — sometimes even more decisive than the cover image. We share 5 high-CTR title formulas that help you write headlines with both substance and magnetism.

docs.title: 4.2 Body Text Rhythm
docs.text: The body is where your core value lives, but if the layout is cluttered and the information density overwhelming, readers will simply scroll away. Learn how paragraph pacing, visual dividers, and strong openings and closings make your text as comfortable to read as breathing.

docs.title: 4.3 Elegant Keyword Embedding
docs.text: Search traffic on Xiaohongshu is too important to ignore. How do you weave keywords into your title and body naturally — without sacrificing readability — so that more people discover your content? This chapter teaches you precision keyword placement.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
Practical Skills

  • Master 5 title formulas so you never stare at a blank headline again
  • Develop a sense of rhythm in body text layout for clean, inviting posts
  • Understand keyword strategy to capture more search traffic

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Start with 4.1 The Art of Titles and learn how to write headlines that are impossible to scroll past.

docs.title: 4.3 Elegant Keyword Embedding
docs.text:
Many creators focus solely on recommendation-feed traffic and overlook a massive secondary channel — search. Xiaohongshu's search engine is growing fast; users actively look up terms like "fall outfits," "affordable skincare," or "small apartment makeover." If your post contains those words, it can surface in search results — and search traffic tends to be more targeted and higher-converting than browse traffic.

The key question is: how do you weave keywords into your copy naturally, without hurting readability, while still being recognized by the algorithm?

docs.title: Why Keywords Matter
docs.text:
The Role of Keywords

  • Search discovery: When a user searches a keyword, your post has a chance to appear in the results
  • Better recommendation targeting: The algorithm uses keywords to understand your content and push it to the right audience
  • Long-tail traffic: A post published months ago can still receive steady exposure through search

On Xiaohongshu, search traffic and recommendation traffic each account for roughly half of total impressions. Ignoring search optimization means giving up half your potential readers.

docs.title: Where to Place Keywords
docs.text:
Different positions carry different weight. Prioritize placing your core keyword where it counts most.

Keyword Weight Hierarchy

Title (highest weight) > First paragraph > Rest of the body > Hashtags

docs.title: Keywords in the Title
docs.text:
The title is the single most important location for search ranking. Your core keyword must appear here.

Examples

  • Want to rank for "Xiaohongshu layout" → Title: "Must-Read | Xiaohongshu Layout Guide for Beginners"
  • Want to rank for "fall outfits" → Title: "This Fall Outfit Is Perfect for Commuting"

docs.title: The First Paragraph
docs.text:
The opening paragraph carries the second-highest weight. Introduce your keyword naturally at the start — the algorithm picks it up and readers immediately understand the topic.

Example

"Today I am sharing a set of super-practical Xiaohongshu layout tips, especially suited for new creators just getting started with their first posts."

docs.title: The Rest of the Body
docs.text: Repeating the keyword throughout the body reinforces topical relevance — but it must read naturally.

docs.title: Hashtags
docs.text: Hashtags supplement your keywords and help the algorithm further categorize your content.

docs.title: Natural Embedding: Weave, Do Not Stack
docs.text:
The biggest keyword mistake is "stuffing" — repeating a keyword like an ad banner. It ruins the reading experience and can even get your post flagged as low-quality content.

Stuffing vs. Natural Embedding

  • Stuffing: "Outfits outfits outfits, today I share outfits, outfit recs, outfit roundup" — reads like spam, and the platform penalizes it
  • Natural embedding: "This fall outfit is perfect for commuting — simple, flattering, and works for every body type" — the keywords "fall outfit" and "commuting" appear naturally inside the sentence

How to Embed Naturally

  • Treat the keyword as part of the sentence, not a standalone element
  • Use synonyms and variations for the same topic: "outfit," "look," "ensemble," "fit"
  • 3-5 appearances of your core keyword per post is plenty — you do not need it in every sentence

Well-Embedded Copy

"Fall is here, so let me share my go-to fall commute outfit. The core of this look is layering — a blazer over a knit top, paired with wide-leg trousers. The overall fit is slimming, and it works from the office straight to dinner."

The keywords "fall commute outfit," "look," and "fit" are spread across different sentences. The text reads smoothly, and the algorithm can still identify the topic.

docs.title: Long-Tail Keyword Strategy: Specific Beats Generic
docs.text:
"Long-tail keywords" are more specific, niche search terms. They have smaller search volumes than broad keywords, but far less competition — making it much easier to rank near the top.

What Are Long-Tail Keywords?

  • Broad keyword: "outfits" — huge search volume, but insanely competitive; a new post has almost no chance of ranking high
  • Long-tail keyword: "petite fall-winter outfits" — moderate search volume, low competition, and the searcher's intent is crystal clear

Long-Tail Keyword Breakdown

Broad Keyword Long-Tail Keywords Outfits petite fall-winter outfits, commute outfit ideas, plus-size slimming outfits Skincare affordable oily-skin skincare, sensitive-skin winter routine, student budget skincare Home decor small-apartment renovation, under-$10K makeover, rental apartment glow-up

How to Find Long-Tail Keywords

  • Open the Xiaohongshu search bar, type a broad keyword, and note the auto-suggested terms
  • Study the titles of viral posts in your niche — what specific phrases do they use?
  • Put yourself in your target reader's shoes — what would they type? The more specific, the better

docs.title: Hashtag Strategy
docs.text:
Hashtags (topic tags) are a key part of your keyword ecosystem. The right combination maximizes your content's exposure.

Hashtag Formula

3-5 precise tags + 1-2 trending tags + 1 category tag

Hashtag Example

Suppose you publish a post about "petite fall-winter outfits":

  • Precise tags: #PetiteOutfits #FallWinterOutfits #CommuteOutfit #HeightBoostingStyle
  • Trending tags: #OOTD #DailyOutfit
  • Category tag: #Fashion

Hashtag Pitfalls

  • Do not rely only on mega-popular tags (like #Outfits) — your post will drown in the flood
  • Do not slap on unrelated tags to chase traffic — the algorithm detects mismatches
  • Keep the total between 5 and 8 tags; too many look cluttered
  • Do not repeat the same word across multiple tags

docs.title: Chapter Recap
docs.text:
Keyword optimization is not a technical exercise — it is an empathy exercise. Put yourself in the reader's seat and ask: if I wanted to find this topic, what would I type? Then weave those words naturally into your title and body.

Practice Exercise

  1. Pick a niche you write about often (e.g., outfits, skincare, food)
  2. Open the Xiaohongshu search bar, type your core keyword, and note 10 auto-suggestions
  3. Choose 2-3 long-tail keywords, then write a title plus three body paragraphs that incorporate them naturally

Back to: Part 4 Overview

docs.title: 4.1 The Art of Titles
docs.text:
Your title is the front door to your post. Users take roughly half a second to decide whether to tap — the title carries just as much weight as the cover image.

5 Tipsto make your posts 10x better Number Title ↑ Why does nobodyread your posts? Pain-Point Title ↑ I finally found...the best palette for fall Curiosity Gap ↑ Basic covervsPremium cover Comparison Title ↑

docs.title: 1. The Number Title
docs.text:
Numbers signal value instantly — "5 tricks" feels more concrete than "several tricks." Place the number at the beginning for maximum impact.

Examples

  • "5 Tricks to Make Your Cover 10x Better"
  • "3 Steps to Perfect Your Color Palette — Even as a Beginner"
  • "8 Must-Have Products Under $15 for Students"

docs.title: 2. The Pain-Point Title
docs.text:
Hit the reader's frustration head-on, making them feel "that is exactly my problem."

Examples

  • "Why Does Nobody Engage with Your Posts?"
  • "Your Layout Always Looks Messy? You Are Overlooking This One Thing"

docs.title: 3. The Curiosity Gap Title
docs.text:
Create a mystery the reader has to resolve by tapping in.

Examples

  • "I Finally Found the Best Layout Method for Beginners..."
  • "This Color Secret — 90% of Creators Have No Idea"

Watch Out

There is a fine line between curiosity and clickbait. Always make sure your content fulfills the title's promise.

docs.title: 4. The Comparison Title
docs.text:
Contrast creates tension — readers want to know "what is the difference?"

Examples

  • "Amateur Cover vs. Premium Cover — Where Is the Gap?"
  • "Beginner Layout vs. Pro Layout — One Detail Makes All the Difference"

docs.title: 5. The List / Label Title
docs.text:
A separator tag tells the reader exactly what the post is about and who it is for.

Examples

  • "Must-Read | Xiaohongshu Layout Guide for Beginners"
  • "Save This | 2024 Fall-Winter Outfit Inspiration Collection"

docs.title: Title Length: Aim for 15-20 Characters
docs.text:
Too short and there is not enough information; too long and the key message gets cut off. Front-load your key information.

Pro Tip

Preview your title on the actual app to make sure important words are not truncated.

docs.title: Titles with Visual Imagery
docs.text:
Titles that evoke vivid pictures are naturally more compelling. Add color ("caramel"), scenes ("date night"), and details ("got asked 3 times").

Compare

  • Low imagery: "Sharing an outfit idea"
  • High imagery: "This caramel-toned outfit got me stopped 3 times on the street for links"

docs.title: Title Pitfalls to Avoid
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Clickbait: Exaggerated title with hollow content leads to unfollows or reports
  • Overly long titles: Once truncated, the meaning is lost
  • Zero information: "Just sharing" gives the reader no reason to tap
  • Title-content mismatch: Tanks the reader experience

docs.title: Chapter Recap
docs.text:
Writing titles is a skill you can practice. Pause on every title that makes you want to tap and ask: which formula did it use?

Practice Exercise

Take your 3 most recent posts and rewrite each title using all 5 formulas. Compare which version pulls you in the most.

Next chapter: 4.2 Body Text Rhythm — learn how to make your post body as comfortable to read as breathing.

docs.title: 5.1 Building Visual Consistency
docs.text:
Open the profile page of a creator you admire and scroll down a few screens. You will notice that the pages that feel truly "beautiful" are rarely about any single stunning image — they feel harmonious, unified, and effortless as a whole.

That is the power of visual consistency. It makes your content look like it was made by one person with a clear vision, not assembled at random.

docs.title: The Three Pillars of Consistency
docs.text: You do not need every post to look identical. You just need to control three core elements:

docs.title: 1. Color Tone
docs.text:
Color tone is the most immediately noticeable aspect of consistency. When all of your posts lean warm, lean cool, or stay within a particular tonal range, your profile grid naturally feels cohesive.

Tone vs. Color

Tone refers to the overall color tendency of an image, not the specific colors used. Two images with completely different color palettes can still look harmonious if their tonal direction is the same — for instance, both slightly warm and slightly desaturated.

docs.title: 2. Typography
docs.text:
If your covers include text, font choice directly impacts recognition. Switching between a rounded sans-serif today, a bold Gothic tomorrow, and a handwritten script the day after creates visual chaos.

Font recommendation

Choose 1–2 fonts as your "signature fonts" — one for headings, one for body or supporting text. Stick with them consistently, and they will become part of your visual fingerprint.

docs.title: 3. Layout Habits
docs.text: Where does the text go? Is the title at the top or centered? How much whitespace is there? These small layout habits accumulate into a recognizable style over time.

docs.title: Fixed Filters and Presets
docs.text:
Adjusting editing parameters from scratch for every photo wastes time and makes consistency nearly impossible. The most efficient approach is to create your own filter preset.

How to do it

In your go-to editing app (Lightroom, VSCO,醒图, etc.), dial in a set of adjustments you like and save them as a preset. For each new photo, apply the preset first, then fine-tune. This gives every image a unified color foundation.

The benefit goes beyond efficiency — fixed presets build visual memory. When followers repeatedly see the same tonal quality in your images, they begin to associate that look with you, even before reading your name.

A word of caution

Presets are not magic. Photos taken under different lighting conditions can look very different even with the same preset applied. Treat your preset as a starting point, not a final step — every image deserves a small round of manual adjustment.

docs.title: Cover Templating
docs.text:
If you publish graphic-and-text notes (as opposed to pure photography), cover templating is a powerful tool for both efficiency and recognition.

"Templating" does not mean every cover looks identical. It means establishing a fixed framework:

  • Fixed font: The title always uses the same typeface
  • Fixed position: The title always sits in the same area (top, center, or bottom)
  • Fixed style: The background treatment is consistent (solid color, gradient, image overlay, etc.)
  • Fixed proportions: The ratio of text to imagery stays relatively stable

Template flexibility

A good template is like a picture frame — the content inside changes, but the frame itself stays stable. This prevents visual fatigue while preserving recognition.

Tools like Canva or 稿定设计 make it easy to create and reuse templates. Each time you publish, you simply swap out the text and imagery — a polished cover in minutes.

docs.title: Profile-Level Cohesion
docs.text:
Many creators focus only on whether each individual post looks good, overlooking a far more important perspective: what does your profile look like as a whole?

Try viewing your profile through the eyes of a stranger:

  1. Open your profile page, hold your phone at arm's length, and look at the overall color blocks — not the details.
  2. Is the color tone unified? Or is it a patchwork of clashing hues?
  3. Is the style consistent? Or does one post look editorial, the next look casual, and the one after that look like a tech review?
  4. Is it recognizable? If someone covered your profile picture, would they still know it is your page?

Common pitfalls

  • Posting everything: Food today, fashion tomorrow, landscapes the next day — with no visual thread tying them together, the profile looks like a grab bag.
  • Inconsistent filters: A different filter every time means the tone swings between warm and cool unpredictably.
  • Jumping cover styles: Some covers are all-text, some are photos, some are collages — visual chaos.

How to improve

If your content genuinely spans multiple topics, you can use a unified tone and cover template as your safety net. Even when subjects differ, a consistent visual framework keeps the profile from feeling scattered.

✗ Inconsistent style ✓ Unified tone

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Visual consistency does not limit your creativity — it gives your creativity a home. Within that framework you can still explore freely, but your content will look more professional and more recognizably you.

Remember three things:

  1. Unify your tone — use a fixed filter preset as your baseline
  2. Unify your fonts and layout — build a cover template
  3. Mind the whole profile — periodically review your grid through a stranger's eyes

Next, we will talk about how to discover a style that is truly your own.

docs.title: 5.3 Style Evolution
docs.text:
Congratulations on finding your style — but the story does not end here.

The colors you loved a year ago might feel "too loud" now. The layout that excited you six months ago might feel a little stale today. That is not a problem — that is growth.

Style is not a monument. It is more like a tree — the roots are stable, but the branches and leaves change with the seasons. In this chapter, we discuss how to let your style grow in a healthy way, rather than becoming rigid or spinning out of control.

docs.title: Style Is Not Meant to Stay the Same
docs.text:
First, let go of a common worry: your style can change, and it should.

Why style changes

  • Your taste is maturing. The more great work you see, the more refined your eye becomes.
  • Your skills are improving. Effects you could not achieve before are now within reach.
  • The platform is evolving. Visual trends on Xiaohongshu shift constantly.
  • You are changing. Your life, interests, and values are always in motion.

Look at the earliest posts of any major creator you admire — they are almost unrecognizable compared to today's work. They, too, evolved step by step.

Two dangerous extremes

  • Never changing: Using the same style you had three years ago, even though it now feels dated and you are bored with it.
  • Changing too fast: A completely new look every month — your followers cannot remember who you are.

docs.title: Gradual Change
docs.text:
The core principle of style evolution is: change only one element at a time.

Think of your style as a house. You can repaint the walls, but do not simultaneously tear off the roof, replace the flooring, and swap out the windows. Change too many things at once and the house becomes unrecognizable.

Examples of gradual change:

Phase Element Changed Elements Kept Step 1 Color tone shifts from warm yellow to cream Font, layout structure, cover template Step 2 Font switches from rounded to thin sans-serif Tone (updated), layout structure, cover template Step 3 Cover template adjusts whitespace ratio Tone, font (updated), overall structure

Leave a buffer between changes

After changing one element, publish at least 5–10 posts before considering the next change. This gives your followers time to adjust and gives you enough data to judge whether the change was actually an improvement.

Think of it as the Ship of Theseus — replace one plank at a time and the ship is still the same ship. Replace every plank at once and you have a different vessel entirely.

docs.title: When to Change
docs.text: Not every feeling of dissatisfaction means you need to overhaul your style. Sometimes you are just tired. But certain signals are worth paying attention to:

docs.title: Aesthetic Fatigue
docs.text:
What it looks like

You open your template and feel "this is so boring." Designing feels like a chore. Publishing brings no sense of accomplishment.

This is the most common signal. Aesthetic fatigue means your taste has outgrown your current style's expressive range. It is time for your style to catch up with your eye.

docs.title: Audience Shift
docs.text: Your follower base is growing and changing. The people who followed you at the beginning may have different aesthetic preferences from those who follow you now. A thoughtful style adjustment can help you resonate more deeply with your current audience.

docs.title: Content Direction Change
docs.text: If your content has gradually shifted from "food reviews" to "lifestyle," or expanded from "product testing" to "personal growth," your original visual style may no longer fit. When the boundaries of your content change, your style needs to expand alongside them.

docs.title: Personal Growth
docs.text:
The most fundamental reason

You have changed. Your taste, your life circumstances, your understanding of what "beauty" means — all of it is constantly evolving. Style is simply the outward expression of those inner changes. Allowing your style to grow with you is the most authentic form of self-respect.

docs.title: Staying Recognizable Through Change
docs.text: Change is healthy, but losing recognition defeats the purpose. The key is to distinguish between core elements and peripheral elements.

docs.title: Core Elements (Keep Stable)
docs.text:
Core elements are the cues your followers rely on to recognize you:

  • Layout structure: Where the title sits, the relationship between text and imagery, the rhythm of whitespace
  • Content tone: Your expressive voice and emotional temperature (warm? sharp? humorous?)
  • Visual signature: A unique, consistent detail you always use (such as a fixed logo position or a particular border style)

docs.title: Peripheral Elements (Iterate Freely)
docs.text:
Peripheral elements are the "clothing" of your style — changing them does not alter the essence:

  • Color tone: Shifting from a warm palette to a cool one
  • Font style: Moving from handwritten to geometric
  • Filter look: Trading a film aesthetic for a bright, airy feel
  • Accent colors: Fine-tuning your dominant hue

A practical test

After making a change, place your new posts side by side with your older ones. If you feel "this is clearly the same person, just leveled up" — you are on the right track. If they look like two completely different creators — you changed too much. Dial it back a little.

docs.title: Document Your Style Journey
docs.text:
One final suggestion: keep a record of how your style evolves.

How to document it

  • Every three months, screenshot your profile's top nine grid
  • Note your style keywords and key editing parameters at that point
  • Write a few sentences about how you felt: What did you enjoy? What felt stale? What did you want to try next?

This is more than a growth archive — it is a treasure chest of future inspiration. When you are unsure where to go next, looking back at where you have been often reveals the way forward.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Style evolution is a journey without a finish line, but it follows a clear method:

  1. Embrace change — style will shift, and it should; that is the natural result of growth
  2. Change gradually — adjust one element at a time; give yourself and your followers room to adapt
  3. Read the signals — aesthetic fatigue, audience shifts, content pivots, personal growth
  4. Protect the core — keep layout structure and content tone stable; iterate freely on color and typography
  5. Document the journey — review periodically and you will see how far you have come

Your style is not something you "find" and then lock away forever. It is your visual self-expression — and you are always growing.

Let your style keep pace with who you are becoming. That is enough.

docs.title: Part 5: Visual Identity
docs.text:
Your posts look good — but can someone recognize them as yours before they even see your profile picture?

Visual identity is your "face" on Xiaohongshu. When a follower scrolls past a cover image and immediately thinks, "Oh, that's from XX" — that is visual recognition. It is not something you are born with; it is something you build, one post at a time.
